content:
The API, the JSON and the Mainframe

8 min read
2 hours ago

--

Quick recap: In the last part, we defined the VSAM, preprocessed the dataset, and loaded the data into the VSAM using COBOL.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The road to JSON
Before jumping into code, let’s understand how to convert VSAM information into JSON format

• The old way: create a fixed format with FILLER fields for every brace, quote and key name. Verbose, frail and tedious to refactor.

• The new way: use JSON GENERATE and some file or variable to store the result.
In modernization fashion we will use the second approach not only because it’s the new one but allows us to be flexible, this link will show all the possibilities JSON GENERATE add to the table.
there is one more thing to define: how are we going to extract the required information? With argument parsing.
In JCL code, we can use pass custom parameters with PARM, as shown in this snippet:
//RUN EXEC PGM=PROG,PARM='OUPARAMETERS'For this project our code will receive three options:

• ‘YEAR=’ — We fix the year and get all the months.

• ‘MONTH=’ — We fix the month and get all years with this month.

• ‘YEARMONTH=’ — Get data from id with YYYYMM convention.
In the best case the id exists for all of them
The Path of Ugly
What happens when our program doesn’t find any year or month, even an id or when it’s not numbers? The idea is simple, return an error json with a suggested error code (404, 500). Here is a snippet showing how it would return to our Express server:
{
"TYPE-INFO": "ERROR",
"REASON": "ID NOT FOUND",
"ERR-CODE": 404
}Note that JSON GENERATE uses COBOL field names as keys, so the Express server will receive these exact keys.
Finally! Code!
With all our ideas and paths mapped it’s time to start structuring the code. First our DATA DIVISION will have a new section the LINKAGE SECTION, this section is responsible to hold parameters and size. Our procedure division needs to use this data, this snippet will show how it’s done:
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 PARMDATA.
05 STRINGLEN PIC S9(4) USAGE COMP.
05 STRINGPARM PIC X(80).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARMDATA.
000-MAIN.
OPEN OUTPUT JSON-FD.
OPEN INPUT CLSTER-FD.
PERFORM PROCESS-ARGS.
010-END.
CLOSE JSON-FD.
CLOSE CLSTER-FD.
GOBACK.Author’s Note: The actual code have 203 lines compared to 75 lines from last part code. I decided to cut and show only relevant informations, there will be a repository at the end with all the code shown in this series.
And our JSON (for error too) will have the following structure in COBOL code:
01 JSON-REC.
05 TYPE-INFO PIC X(5).
05 TOTAL-SCORE PIC 9(18) VALUE ZERO.
05 TOTAL-COMMENTS PIC 9(18) VALUE ZERO.
05 AVG-SCORE PIC 9(11)V99 VALUE ZERO.
05 AVG-COMMENT PIC 9(11)V99 VALUE ZERO.
01 ERROR-JSON.
05 TYPE-INFO PIC X(5) VALUE 'ERROR'.
05 REASON PIC X(25).
05 ERR-CODE PIC 9(3).PROCESS-ARGS will be responsible to deal with 4 scenarios

• Empty parameters

• Not YEAR, MONTH or YEARMONTH parameter

• Is a valid parameter but don’t matches it’s size requirement (4 for year, 2 for month and 6 for yearmonth)

• It matches the size but is not a number
The last scenario is achievable with IS NOT NUMERIC inside a IF. Look at this snippet that deal with the YEAR parameter
IF STRINGPARM(1:5) EQUAL TO 'YEAR='
SUBTRACT 5 FROM STRINGLEN
IF STRINGLEN NOT EQUAL TO 4
MOVE 'YEAR MUST HAVE 4 DIGITS' TO REASON
MOVE 500 TO ERR-CODE
PERFORM GEN-ERROR-JSON
EXIT PARAGRAPH
END-IF
MOVE STRINGPARM(6:4) TO WS-TMP-STR
DISPLAY WS-TMP-STR
IF WS-TMP-STR(1:4) IS NOT NUMERIC
MOVE 'EXPECTED NUMBERS' TO REASON
MOVE 500 TO ERR-CODE
PERFORM GEN-ERROR-JSON
EXIT PARAGRAPH
END-IF
MOVE WS-TMP-STR(1:4) TO WS-YEAR
PERFORM GET-ALL-SAME-YEAR
EXIT PARAGRAPH
END-IF.For an equivalent in JS:
if (STRINGPARM.slice(0,5) === "YEAR="){
const len = STRINGPARM.length-5
if (len != 4) {
ERROR_JSON["REASON"] = "YEAR MUST HAVE 4 DIGITS"
ERROR_JSON["ERR-CODE"] = 500
genErrorJson()
return
}
const tmpStr = STRINGPARM.slice(5)
const year = Number(tmpStr)
if (isNaN(year)) {
ERROR_JSON["REASON"] = "EXPECTED NUMBERS"
ERROR_JSON["ERR-CODE"] = 500
genErrorJson()
return;
}
getAllSameYear(year)
}Each parameter follows the same validation pattern. ANY-VAL controls whether data was found, if found then write the result into JSON, else write the error JSON.
Our function to calculate the average in our code is:
CALC-AVG.
DIVIDE TMP-AVG-SCORE BY WS-TOTAL-CTR
GIVING AVG-SCORE ROUNDED.
DIVIDE TMP-AVG-COMMENTS BY WS-TOTAL-CTR
GIVING AVG-COMMENT ROUNDED.COBOL DIVIDE with ROUNDED have the same effect as:
const c = Math.round(a / b)JSON GENERATE has a very powerful keyword the SUPPRESS keyword, it’s responsible to tell the compiler to not serialize certain fields. We will be using it on the GET-ID, where we don’t need the average of score and comments.
The snippet for this part:
GET-ID.
PERFORM READ-VSAM.
IF ANY-VAL NOT EQUAL TO 'Y'
MOVE 'ID NOT FOUND' TO REASON
MOVE 404 TO ERR-CODE
PERFORM GEN-ERROR-JSON
ELSE
MOVE 'ID' TO TYPE-INFO IN JSON-REC
MOVE CLSTER-SCORE TO TOTAL-SCORE
MOVE CLSTER-COMMENT TO TOTAL-COMMENTS
JSON GENERATE JSON-DATA FROM JSON-REC
NAME OF JSON-REC IS OMITTED
SUPPRESS AVG-SCORE AVG-COMMENT
END-JSON
WRITE JSON-DATA
END-IF.Without NAME OF JSON-REC IS OMITTED and SUPRESS, we would recieve something like:
{
"JSON-REC": {
"TYPE-INFO":"YEAR",
"TOTAL-SCORE":349,
"TOTAL-COMMENTS":86,
"AVG-SCORE":0,
"AVG-COMMENT":0
}
}As we can see JSON GENERATE is very flexible allowing to ommit names and fields
For now we will only compile it. We will explore more about how to get data later:
//COMPHN JOB (001),'COMPILE HN EXTRACTOR',NOTIFY=&SYSUID
//COMPILE EXEC IGWCL
//COBOL.SYSIN DD DSN=&SYSUID..CBL(GETHN),DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=&SYSUID..LOAD(GETHN),DISP=SHR
For a few more lines of code
Now it’s time to write the last lines of code, but as in this article fashion, we must define the routes, there will be three paths:

• /api/hn/year/{year} — For our “YEAR=” parameter

• /api/hn/month/{month} — For our “MONTH=” parameter

• /api/hn/year/{year}/{month} — For our “YEARMONTH=” parameter
Author’s note: I decided to follow the third path as year/{year}/{month} because is a more specific version of year, instead of getting data from all months we get data from one month only.
All of them will only have one allowed method: “GET”.
ZOWE is able to translate JCL variables before submission, just need to prefix with & like &PARAM, and pass to code. Our JCL will have a new symbol called PARAMS and this is the code:
//GETHN JOB (001),'GET DATA FROM HN VSAM',
// MSGLEVEL=(1,1),NOTIFY=&SYSUID
//RUN EXEC PGM=GETHN,PARM=&PARAMS
//STEPLIB DD DSN=&SYSUID..LOAD
//RESULT DD SYSOUT=*
//CLSTER DD DSN=&SYSUID..HN.CLSTER,DISP=SHARE
//SYSOUT DD SYSOUT=*,OUTLIM=15000
//SYSUDUMP DD DUMMYOutlim limits the amount of lines to 15000.
DD SYSOUT is used to print information to save data in the system spool, similar to Linux stdout, where we can retrive later.
To get data using javascript we will have to:

• Login with our credentials in ZOWE (assuming is in a profile saved somewhere on the operating system).

• Send JCL code in a String with JCL Symbols to ZOWE to solve.

• Wait to process

• Get all spools and filter for desired spool

• If the desired spool is found, parse it’s data and return a JSON. If not found then an error json is returned.
In code:
const session = await (async () => {
const profInfo = new ProfileInfo("zowe")
await profInfo.readProfilesFromDisk()
const zosmfMergedArgs = profInfo.mergeArgsForProfile(profInfo.getDefaultProfile("zosmf"), {getSecureVals: true})
const session = ProfileInfo.createSession(zosmfMergedArgs.knownArgs, {type: "basic"})
return session
})()const getResult = async (value) => {
const job = await SubmitJobs.submitJclCommon(session, {
jcl: jclString.replace('&PARAMS', value),
})
const jobResult = await MonitorJobs.waitForStatusCommon(session, {
jobname: job.jobname,
jobid: job.jobid,
status: "OUTPUT",
attempts: 20
})
const spoolFiles = await GetJobs.getSpoolFiles(session, jobResult.jobname, jobResult.jobid)
for (const spool of spoolFiles) {
if (spool.ddname === datasetName) {
const val = await GetJobs.getSpoolContentById(
session, jobResult.jobname,
jobResult.jobid, spool.id, "UTF-8"
)
const trimmed = val.trimEnd();
const regMatch = trimmed.match(/\{.*\}/s);
if (regMatch) {
const json = JSON.parse(regMatch[0]);
return json
}
}
}
return {
"TYPE-INFO": "ERROR",
"REASON": `DATASET ${datasetName} NOT FOUND.`,
"ERR-CODE": 500
}
}
Due to an encoding issue in the spool output, invisible characters may be introduced during the conversion process. Instead of parsing the entire response directly, a regular expression is used to extract the JSON object, ensuring that we parse only valid JSON data.
Now our routes will have to deal with the returns, the logic is the same for the three routes, giving us this snippet:
const handleRes = (res, jclResult) => {
if (jclResult["TYPE-INFO"] === "ERROR") {
res.status(jclResult["ERR-CODE"])
delete jclResult["ERR-CODE"]
res.send(jclResult)
return
}
res.status(200)
res.send(jclResult)
}app.get("/api/hn/year/:year", async (req, res) => {
const year = req.params.year
const jclResult = await getResult(`'YEAR=${year}'`)
handleRes(res, jclResult)
})
app.get("/api/hn/month/:month", async (req, res) => {
const month = req.params.month
const jclResult = await getResult(`'MONTH=${month}'`)
handleRes(res, jclResult)
})
app.get("/api/hn/year/:year/:month", async (req, res) => {
const year = req.params.year
const month = req.params.month
const jclResult = await getResult(`'YEARMONTH=${year}${month}'`)
handleRes(res, jclResult)
})
A test to end the day
I made a youtube video with the result (1 min):
Improvements and Next Steps
This tutorial series is focused on mainframe technologies and concepts: modeling, preprocessing data, parsing CSV to a VSAM and running BATCH COBOL to get data. But API aspects were intentionally left out of scope.
For the API Side:

• Rate limiting — A single user can flood the JES queue with many requests.

• Redis for caching — Some endpoint responses could be cached to reduce mainframe requests.

• Authentication Method (like JWT) — Standard for production level APIs to have some method, it ensures only select user to modify and access data.
For the Mainframe Side:

• Use Online COBOL — With CICS, the mainframe would act as a real service instead of a batch processor.
For Both Sides:

• Endpoints for inserting and updating VSAM records — If you get data from other years or months there is no way to do in the current project.
Conclusion
Mainframe modernization is something that is happening in the tech world, protocols like SOAP and REST already bridge mainframe services to modern applications. This tutorial series focuses on understanding the key aspects of a modernization pipeline.
Because of mainframe history and context, many of old times limitations are still reflected (like 80 columns wide documents). Understanding these aspects and general concepts makes the learning curve quite steep. But once you understand these concepts, even the oldest mainframe system becomes navigable and the architecture starts to make sense.
The full project is available on GitHub, where I’ll continue to refactor and add new functionality: github.com/HedroPedro/Hacker-News-API
Next steps
For the next project I will compare two assembly implementations for the same purpose, a word and character counter in Linux x86–64 assembly and z/Architecture assembly for z/OS.
Stay tuned!
Acknowledgements

If you want to get hands-on with a real mainframe, check out IBM Z Xplore at ibm.biz/refer-zxp. If you enjoyed this series and want to support it, use the Ambassador Referral (a pink box) and search for Pedro Henrique de Oliveira, it means a lot!
